---
title: "Vectors and Derivatives"
---

## Vectors in 2-D

A vector $\mathbf{r}(t)$ traces a curve in space as $t$ varies.
Its **velocity** is the derivative $\dot{\mathbf{r}}(t) = d\mathbf{r}/dt$.

## Python example – parametric curve and velocity vectors

The code below plots the Lissajous curve
$$\mathbf{r}(t) = (\cos t,\; \sin 2t)$$
together with numerically-computed velocity vectors sampled every few points.

```{python}
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

t = np.linspace(0, 2 * np.pi, 400)
x = np.cos(t)
y = np.sin(2 * t)

# Numerical derivative
dt = t[1] - t[0]
vx = np.gradient(x, dt)
vy = np.gradient(y, dt)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6, 4))
ax.plot(x, y, label=r"$\mathbf{r}(t)$")
ax.quiver(
    x[::25], y[::25],
    vx[::25], vy[::25],
    angles="xy", scale_units="xy", scale=10, width=0.004,
    color="C1", label=r"$\dot{\mathbf{r}}(t)$",
)
ax.set_aspect("equal", "box")
ax.set_title("Lissajous curve and velocity vectors")
ax.grid(True, alpha=0.3)
ax.legend()
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

## Key identities

| Symbol | Meaning |
|--------|---------|
| $\mathbf{r}(t)$ | Position vector |
| $\dot{\mathbf{r}}(t)$ | Velocity vector |
| $\ddot{\mathbf{r}}(t)$ | Acceleration vector |

> **Note:** In three dimensions, $\mathbf{r} = (x, y, z)$ and each component
> is differentiated independently.
